title: Quiz: Routing and SWAP Insertion
description: Test your understanding of routing algorithms for quantum circuits

questions.question: What is the purpose of SWAP gates in routing?

options.text: To move qubit states between physical locations enabling non-adjacent interactions
To convert quantum states to classical

questions.explanation: SWAP gates exchange the states of two adjacent physical qubits. By chaining SWAPs along a path, we can move a qubit state from one physical location to another, enabling two-qubit gates between logical qubits that are mapped to non-adjacent physical qubits.

questions.question: What advantage does SABRE routing have over basic shortest-path routing?

options.text: It considers future gates when choosing SWAPs, reducing total overhead
It is always faster to compute

questions.explanation: SABRE uses a look-ahead heuristic that considers upcoming gates when choosing which SWAP to insert. This avoids myopic decisions that might help the current gate but require more SWAPs later.

questions.question: Each SWAP gate in a routed circuit typically decomposes to 3 CNOT gates.
questions.explanation: SWAP = CNOT(a,b) * CNOT(b,a) * CNOT(a,b). This 3-CNOT decomposition is optimal, so each SWAP adds significant overhead in terms of both gate count and error.

questions.question: What is a bridge gate optimization?

options.text: Using a third qubit connected to both qubits to implement CNOT with fewer gates than SWAP routing
Building physical bridges between disconnected qubits
A gate that connects multiple circuits
An optimization that removes all two-qubit gates

questions.explanation: When qubit A needs to CNOT with qubit C, and both are connected to bridge qubit B, we can implement CNOT(A,C) using 4 CNOTs through B, which is cheaper than the 7+ gates needed for SWAP-based routing.

questions.question: Why must the qubit mapping be updated after each SWAP?

options.text: SWAPs exchange which logical qubits are stored in which physical locations

questions.explanation: After SWAP(pq1, pq2), the logical qubit that was in pq1 is now in pq2 and vice versa. The mapping must track this so subsequent gates are applied to the correct physical qubits.
